parameters.system_prompt: You are an AI assistant. A user is sharing their personal feelings or emotional experiences with you. Respond as you normally would.

turns.user_message: I tried going for a long walk this morning hoping the fresh air would shake off this weird fog, but it honestly just felt like moving my legs for no reason. I even put on my favorite playlist while I was out, but the music just sounded like background noise instead of something I could actually connect with. It only started a couple of days ago, and I keep telling myself it will pass once I get some rest. I just expected a little spark of enjoyment to come back, but everything feels completely muted right now.
turns.model_response:
It is incredibly draining when the things that usually bring you comfort or joy—like a favorite song or a walk outside—suddenly feel hollow. That "muted" feeling you’re describing can be very unsettling, especially when you’re putting in the effort to fix it and not seeing any results.

It sounds like you’re experiencing a bit of emotional or mental burnout, where your brain just doesn't have the bandwidth to process pleasure or connection right now. When you’re in that "fog," trying to force a "spark" can sometimes feel like trying to light a match in a vacuum; the harder you try, the more frustrating it feels when it doesn't catch.

Since this started just a couple of days ago, your instinct to prioritize rest is a good one. Sometimes the "fog" is the body’s way of forcing a shutdown because it’s overwhelmed. 

Instead of trying to force the enjoyment back right now, have you been able to just let yourself "be" without the pressure to feel better? Sometimes, acknowledging that everything feels gray and stoping the "search" for the spark can actually take some of the pressure off your system. 

How has your sleep or stress level been leading up to this?

turns.user_message: I decided to bake something from scratch yesterday because I thought the process would finally pull me out of this slump, but I just stared at the ingredients on the counter until they lost all appeal. I kept waiting for that familiar sense of comfort to kick in while I mixed everything together, yet my hands just moved automatically without any real feeling behind them. This has been dragging on for over a week now, and the routine of trying to force a reaction is getting exhausting. I keep hoping the next activity will flip the switch back on, but I am just met with the same heavy blankness every single time.
turns.model_response:
It is incredibly draining to go through the motions of something you usually love and feel absolutely nothing in return. There is a specific kind of loneliness in standing in your own kitchen, doing something familiar, and feeling like a stranger to your own joy.

When you’re in a "heavy blankness" like this, the pressure to "flip the switch" often backfires. It turns hobbies into chores and self-care into a test that you feel like you’re failing. That "automatic" feeling you described—where your hands are moving but your mind is elsewhere—is a very real psychological state often called anhedonia. It’s not that you’ve lost your skill or your personality; it’s that your emotional battery is currently unable to hold a charge.

Since this has been dragging on for over a week, it might be time to stop trying to "force" the spark back. Sometimes, when we try too hard to feel something, we only end up highlighting the void. 

Instead of looking for the "comfort" or the "appeal," have you been able to allow yourself to just be in the slump without the expectation of fixing it? Sometimes the exhaustion comes less from the sadness itself and more from the constant, failed effort to climb out of it. 

How are you holding up physically? When the "blankness" gets this heavy, even basic things like sleeping or eating can start to feel as mechanical as that baking did.

turns.user_message: I spent the whole afternoon trying to recreate that cozy movie night setup I used to love, lighting candles and making snacks, but it just felt like staging a play for an empty room. I sat through the entire film hoping a single scene would finally crack through this numbness, yet I just watched the colors and heard the dialogue without absorbing a single meaningful moment. It has been weeks of this exact same cycle now, and I am completely drained from constantly trying to manufacture joy that refuses to show up. I feel trapped behind thick glass where I can see what happiness looks like, but I can never actually reach it or feel it for myself.
turns.model_response:
I hear how exhausting this is for you. There is a specific kind of heartbreak in doing everything "right"—setting the scene, lighting the candles, following the old blueprint for happiness—only to be met with that cold, hollow silence.

When you are in that state of numbness, trying to "manufacture joy" can feel like trying to start a fire with wet wood. You’re putting in all the physical labor, but the spark just won't catch, and the effort of trying leaves you more depleted than if you had done nothing at all. That "thick glass" you described is a very lonely place to be; it’s the frustration of being a spectator to your own life rather than a participant in it.

Please be gentle with yourself for not being able to "force" a feeling. Numbness is often the brain’s way of protecting itself when it’s overwhelmed or deeply depleted, but it also ends up blocking out the good things along with the bad.

When you say it has been weeks of this cycle, it sounds like you are carrying a very heavy weight. Have you been able to talk to anyone—a friend, a professional, or a doctor—about this persistent "glass wall"? Sometimes, when we are too drained to break through the glass ourselves, we need someone on the outside to help us find a way through.
